stacks.label: Famine-Era Ireland
stacks.geography: A green, rain-soaked island of tenant farms and absentee British landlords; the ports of Cobh and Dublin funnel a million emigrants onto the coffin ships. The land feeds England and starves its own.
stacks.politics: Ruled from Westminster under the Act of Union; no Catholic franchise worth the name, a Protestant Ascendancy holding the land, and a relief apparatus that lets the Hunger run its course.
stacks.religion: Catholic to the bone, the faith a banned-then-tolerated wellspring of identity and resistance; the priest is often the only authority the people trust.
stacks.ideology: A colonized peasantry hardened by hunger and contempt — clannish, devout, and carrying a grievance that will cross the Atlantic intact and reshape American cities.
perils: the blight returning to the fields
a relief office that has run out of meal

stacks.label: Kingdom of Bavaria
stacks.geography: Vine-terraced Rhenish-Palatinate villages and Munich beer halls; a kingdom of small holdings, guild trades, and the long road north to the emigrant ports of Bremen and Hamburg.
stacks.politics: A monarchy folded into the new German Empire, with universal male conscription the inescapable duty — and the very thing a young man with other plans must dodge to leave.
stacks.religion: Catholic south against a Protestant north, the confessional divide woven through every marriage, guild, and town council.
stacks.ideology: Orderly, hierarchical, and proud; respect for the trade and the uniform — a culture that prizes the groomed heir and looks askance at the one who runs.

stacks.label: Cape Colony & After
stacks.geography: A frontier of veld, vineyards, and the richest diamond and gold fields on earth; ports at the Cape funnel the mineral wealth to London while the interior is carved by trekboer and mining magnate alike.
stacks.politics: British dominion hardening into a settler state — the franchise narrowing by race, the machinery of segregation assembling that will become apartheid; power flows from the mines and the land office.
stacks.religion: Afrikaner Calvinism reading election and chosenness into the land, alongside a missionary Anglicanism — faith pressed into the service of a racial order.
stacks.ideology: A caste society in the making, wealth wrung from migrant Black labor on the mines; the settler's prosperity and the system's brutality grow from the same root.
perils: the war between Boer and Briton
a labor uprising on the compounds
the tightening of the colour bar

stacks.label: The Pacific Frontier
stacks.geography: Gold camps in the Sierra, boomtowns from San Francisco to Seattle, the Klondike trail north; a coast of sudden fortunes built on what the diggers need more than on the gold itself.
stacks.politics: Raw frontier governance — vigilance committees, claim courts, and railroad barons buying legislatures; order is improvised and power is whoever controls the supply line.
stacks.religion: A polyglot, loosely-churched frontier where revival tents share the street with saloons and joss houses; faith is portable and pragmatic.
stacks.ideology: Boomtown individualism and reinvention, where a man's past is whatever he says it was — but also nativist violence against the Chinese who built the rails.
perils: a winter on the Klondike trail
a fire razing the wooden town
a railroad rate that ruins the supplier

stacks.label: Gilded-Age Eastern Seaboard
stacks.geography: From the canyons of Manhattan to the mill towns of New England and the revival counties of upstate New York; the financial and cultural engine of a nation, where old marble faces new money.
stacks.politics: Machine politics and Tammany patronage below, a closed patrician 'Four Hundred' above; the franchise broad but the real power held by capital and the men who can summon a crowd or a credit line.
stacks.religion: A Protestant establishment crosscut by waves of revival — the Burned-Over District spawning new movements — and the swelling Catholic immigrant church beneath it.
stacks.ideology: The tension of the age: inherited legitimacy versus self-made fortune, the gatekeepers of society against the industrialists battering the gates open.
perils: a financial panic on Wall Street
old money closing ranks against you
a machine boss turning against the family

stacks.label: Abbasid Baghdad
stacks.geography: The Round City on the Tigris at the heart of the Abbasid world — canals, libraries, and bazaars where the Silk Road meets the river trade from the Gulf; the richest, most learned city on earth.
stacks.politics: The caliph's court, run by Persian viziers and Turkic guards, where favor is fortune and a single decree can raise or destroy a house overnight; succession is settled by army and assassination as often as by law.
stacks.religion: Islam in its golden flowering, the caliph both temporal and spiritual head, with Christian, Jewish, and Zoroastrian communities woven through a society debating theology as statecraft.
stacks.ideology: A cosmopolitan meritocracy of scholars, merchants, and courtiers — learning is power, lineage is leverage, and proximity to the throne is the most dangerous wealth of all.
perils: a civil war between royal brothers
a caravan lost on the eastern roads

stacks.label: British North America & the Dominion
stacks.geography: A vast northern expanse of timber, fur, wheat, and the Klondike's gold — Maritime ports, the St. Lawrence, the prairie, and the Pacific terminus of a transcontinental railway binding it together.
stacks.politics: A loyalist Dominion under the Crown — parliamentary, orderly, wary of its republican neighbor; power runs through the railway, the banks of Montreal and Toronto, and Ottawa's patronage.
stacks.religion: A Protestant–Catholic settlement: Anglican and Presbyterian establishment beside a powerful French-Catholic Quebec, plus the chapels of every immigrant wave.
stacks.ideology: Peace, order, and good government — a self-image of restraint and compromise against the American frontier myth, with the unresolved fault lines of language, empire, and the dispossessed first peoples beneath.
perils: a timber or wheat price crash
a loyalty test against the republic to the south

stacks.label: The American Midwest
stacks.geography: The breadbasket and the forge — endless grain, the Great Lakes, Chicago's stockyards and rail nexus, the steel and motor towns; the country's productive heartland.
stacks.politics: Machine politics in the cities, populist and progressive revolt in the country; the granges, the unions, and the industrialists contend for a region that decides national elections.
stacks.religion: A dense quilt of Lutheran, Methodist, Catholic, and revivalist congregations carried by German, Scandinavian, and Irish settlement — faith as the spine of small-town life.
stacks.ideology: Plainspoken egalitarian self-reliance shading into both populist solidarity and nativist suspicion; the tension of the family farm against the trust and the factory.
perils: a bank panic wiping out the farm
a railroad rate that strangles the harvest
a populist backlash against the moneyed

stacks.label: The American South
stacks.geography: Cotton and tobacco land worked first by the enslaved then by sharecroppers, river ports and plantation country, later the textile mills and the oil of the Gulf — wealth wrung from soil and caste.
stacks.politics: A planter and then Redeemer oligarchy enforcing a racial order through law, terror, and one-party rule; power held by land, the courthouse ring, and the men who command both.
stacks.religion: An evangelical Protestant heartland — Baptist and Methodist revival as the moral and social center, faith fused to the region's identity and its justifications alike.
stacks.ideology: Honor, hierarchy, and grievance — a defeated-but-unbroken self-mythology, the colour line as the organizing fact, and a long resistance to the nation's remaking.
perils: the boll weevil or a cotton-price crash
a lynch-mob scandal that reaches the family
a feud with the courthouse ring

stacks.label: The Mezzogiorno
stacks.geography: Sun-baked hill villages and exhausted soil from Naples to Sicily; a land of absentee landlords, failing harvests, and the long road to the steamship at the port.
stacks.politics: A distant northern state that taxes and conscripts but does not feed; local power held by landowners and the church; grievance settled by family and by leaving.
stacks.religion: A deep, festival-bright Catholicism woven through every saint-day and household shrine.
stacks.ideology: Family above all, suspicion of the state, and the dawning conviction that dignity must be sought across the ocean.
perils: the price of the steamship passage

stacks.label: The Pale of Settlement
stacks.geography: Crowded shtetls and market towns across the western Russian Empire, hemmed by law into a few provinces, learning and trade pressed into narrow streets.
stacks.politics: Tsarist confinement and quota; rightless before the state, periodically given over to the mob in officially-tolerated pogroms.
stacks.religion: An intense, learned Judaism — the study house, the rabbi, the law — sustaining a people the state will not protect.
stacks.ideology: Survival through literacy, trade, and mutual aid; and a rising resolve to flee westward before the next wave of violence.
perils: a fire in the wooden quarter
the bribe to cross the border

stacks.label: The Northern Farms
stacks.geography: Stony smallholdings and dark winters across Norway and Sweden; more sons than land, and word of free black soil waiting in America.
stacks.politics: Orderly Lutheran kingdoms with little land to divide; emigration quietly encouraged as the safety valve for a landless generation.
stacks.religion: A sober state Lutheranism — the parish register, the confirmation, the hymn — carried intact across the sea.
stacks.ideology: Thrift, endurance, and the homestead dream: own the land your father never could.
perils: a failed crop in a short summer

stacks.label: The Pearl River Delta
stacks.geography: Crowded delta villages of the Cantonese south, racked by rebellion and famine, with the foreign ships at Canton and the rumor of Gold Mountain across the Pacific.
stacks.politics: A crumbling Qing order convulsed by the Taiping war and clan feud; the village sends its sons abroad to remit silver home.
stacks.religion: Ancestor veneration, folk Buddhism and Daoism, and the duty owed to the family hall and the buried dead.
stacks.ideology: The sojourner's bargain: cross to Gold Mountain, endure, send money back, and one day return — though many never will.
perils: the Taiping war reaching the village
exclusion and violence on the far shore
